chapter_title: IC-38 General: Full Syllabus Mock Test 1

questions.text: What is the name of the first Fire Insurance Company in the world?
questions.explanation: The 'Fire Office' was the first fire insurance company in the world, established in 1667 after the Great Fire of London.

questions.text: Which of the following is the regulator for the insurance industry in India?
questions.explanation: The Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India (IRDAI) is the statutory body responsible for regulating and promoting the insurance industry in India.

questions.text: By transferring risk to the insurer, it becomes possible to:
options: Enjoy a fire in the factory
Enjoy peace of mind and plan business effectively
questions.explanation: Insurance provides security against financial loss, allowing individuals and businesses to operate with peace of mind.

questions.text: In the insurance context, 'risk retention' indicates a situation where:
options: Possibility of loss or damage is not there
Loss producing event has no value
One decides to bear the risk and its effects
questions.explanation: Risk retention occurs when an individual or business chooses to self-insure or personally bear the financial consequences of a risk.

questions.text: Risk transfer through risk pooling is called:
questions.explanation: Insurance is the process of pooling risks from many individuals to provide protection against the losses of a few.

questions.text: Insurance is a method of sharing the losses of:
options: The gains of a few to the many
questions.explanation: The basic principle of insurance is that the losses of a few are shared by the many who contribute to the insurance pool.

questions.text: Before acceptance of a risk, the insurer arranges a survey and inspection of the property to:
options: Assess the risk for rating purposes
Find out how the insured purchased the property
Find out whether other insurers have also inspected the property
Find out whether neighboring property also can be insured
questions.explanation: Surveys and inspections help underwriters evaluate the risk level to determine the appropriate premium and policy terms.

questions.text: The principle of utmost good faith is also known by the Latin term:
questions.explanation: Uberrimae Fides is the Latin term for Utmost Good Faith, requiring full disclosure of all material facts by both parties.

questions.text: Which principle ensures that the insured is restored to the same financial position as before the loss?
questions.explanation: The Principle of Indemnity states that insurance is meant to compensate for the actual loss, not to allow the insured to profit.

questions.text: The consideration paid by the insured to the insurer for the insurance cover is called:
questions.explanation: Premium is the monetary consideration paid by the policyholder to keep the insurance policy active.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a standard general insurance policy?
questions.explanation: An Endowment Policy is a life insurance product, not a general insurance product.

questions.text: What is a proposal form?
options: A formal document issued after a claim
A formal request for insurance cover by the proposer
questions.explanation: The proposal form is the primary document where the applicant provides details required by the insurer to assess the risk.

questions.text: What does a 'cover note' indicate?
options: Temporary proof of insurance cover before the policy is issued
questions.explanation: A cover note is a temporary document issued by the insurer that provides proof of insurance until the formal policy document is prepared.

questions.text: The principle of subrogation is applicable to:
questions.explanation: Subrogation applies only to contracts of indemnity (like fire or motor insurance) to prevent the insured from recovering more than the actual loss.

questions.text: The principle of contribution applies when:
options: There are multiple insurance policies covering the same risk
The premium is paid in installments
questions.explanation: Contribution ensures that if multiple policies exist for the same risk, all insurers share the loss proportionately.

questions.text: Proximate cause means:
options: The most distant cause of loss
The active, efficient cause that sets in motion a chain of events bringing about a result
The deliberate cause by the insured
questions.explanation: Proximate Cause (Causa Proxima) is the immediate and effective cause of a loss, which determines whether the loss is covered by the policy.

questions.text: Which of the following is considered an 'Act of God' peril?
questions.explanation: Natural disasters like earthquakes, floods, and lightning are classified as 'Acts of God' (force majeure) in insurance.

questions.text: In motor insurance, 'Third Party' refers to:
options: Anyone other than the insured and the insurer
questions.explanation: In an insurance contract, the Insurer is the 1st party, the Insured is the 2nd party, and any other affected person is the 3rd party.

questions.text: What does 'OD' stand for in motor insurance?
questions.explanation: Own Damage (OD) cover protects the policyholder's own vehicle against damage due to accidents, fire, theft, or natural calamities.

questions.text: Health insurance policies generally do NOT cover:
questions.explanation: Cosmetic or aesthetic surgeries are typically excluded from health insurance unless required due to an accident or injury.

questions.text: The standard grace period for the renewal of a health insurance policy is typically:
questions.explanation: A grace period of 30 days is usually allowed to renew a health policy without losing continuity benefits.

questions.text: A 'Family Floater' health insurance policy covers:
options: Multiple family members under a single shared sum insured
Only senior citizens in the family
questions.explanation: A family floater allows all family members to be covered under one policy, sharing a single pool of sum insured.

questions.text: Marine cargo insurance covers risks mainly during:
questions.explanation: Marine cargo insurance provides protection for goods while they are in transit by sea, air, or land.

questions.text: Fidelity guarantee insurance protects an employer against:
options: Financial loss caused by fraud or dishonesty of employees
Loss of profit due to fire
questions.explanation: Fidelity Guarantee covers losses sustained by the employer due to acts of forgery, embezzlement, or theft by employees.

questions.text: Who is the primary beneficiary in a Keyman insurance policy?
questions.explanation: Keyman insurance is taken by a business to protect itself against financial loss resulting from the death of a key employee.

questions.text: Burglary insurance policies usually require proof of:
options: Forcible and violent entry or exit
questions.explanation: Standard burglary policies define the act as involving forcible and violent entry into or exit from the premises.

questions.text: The Insurance Ombudsman in the insurance sector can resolve disputes up to the value of:
questions.explanation: The Insurance Ombudsman has the authority to award compensation up to a maximum of Rs. 30 Lakhs.

questions.text: The time limit for filing a complaint with the Insurance Ombudsman is within:
options: 30 days of rejection of the claim
6 months of rejection of the claim
One year of rejection of the claim by the insurer
Three years of rejection of the claim
questions.explanation: A complaint must be filed with the Ombudsman within 12 months after the insurer has rejected the representation.

questions.text: In health insurance, what is a 'waiting period'?
options: The time taken to issue the policy
The time taken to settle a claim
The time during which specific diseases or conditions are not covered
The time given to pay the premium
questions.explanation: Waiting periods apply to pre-existing diseases or specific surgeries where coverage starts only after a certain duration (e.g., 2-4 years).

questions.text: What is a 'deductible' in an insurance policy?
options: An amount discounted from the premium
The initial amount of the claim that must be borne by the insured
questions.explanation: A deductible is a fixed amount that the policyholder must pay towards a claim before the insurer covers the rest.

questions.text: Which insurance is mandatory for all motor vehicles plying on public roads in India?
questions.explanation: Under the Motor Vehicles Act, Third Party Liability insurance is legally mandatory for all vehicles used in public spaces.

questions.text: The concept of 'Insurable Interest' must exist at what time in general insurance?
options: Only at the time of inception
Only at the time of claim
At both inception and at the time of the claim
It is not required in general insurance
questions.explanation: In general insurance (like fire or motor), the insured must have an insurable interest both when buying the policy and when a loss occurs.

questions.text: What is the standard duration of a basic fire insurance policy?
questions.explanation: Standard fire insurance policies are usually issued for a period of 12 months and renewed annually.

questions.text: TPA in health insurance stands for:
questions.explanation: TPAs are entities licensed by IRDAI to provide administrative services like claim processing and cashless approvals for health insurers.

questions.text: What does 'Cashless Facility' mean in health insurance?
options: The insured does not need to pay premium in cash
The hospital bills are directly settled by the insurer or TPA up to the approved limit
No money is involved in the insurance contract
The claim is paid directly in cash to the insured
questions.explanation: Cashless facility allows the insured to receive treatment at network hospitals without paying the bills upfront (except for non-medical expenses).

questions.text: Professional Indemnity insurance is usually taken by:
questions.explanation: Professional Indemnity (PI) covers legal liability arising from errors, omissions, or negligence in professional services.

questions.text: What does 'Endorsement' mean in an insurance policy?
options: A promotional advertisement for the policy
A document attached to the policy that modifies or alters its original terms
questions.explanation: An endorsement is a formal amendment to the policy document to record changes like address updates or sum insured revisions.

questions.text: Moral hazard refers to:
options: Physical conditions that increase the chance of loss
The attitude, character, or behavior of the insured that increases the chance of loss
The ethical guidelines of the insurance company
questions.explanation: Moral hazard relates to the mental attitude or character of the insured, such as carelessness or dishonest intent to stage a loss.

questions.text: Physical hazard refers to:
options: Physical characteristics of the risk that increase the likelihood of loss
questions.explanation: Physical hazards are tangible factors, such as using flammable materials in a building or poor brakes in a car.

questions.text: Which document contains the complete terms, conditions, and exclusions of the insurance contract?
questions.explanation: The Policy Document is the formal legal evidence of the insurance contract between the insurer and the insured.

questions.text: What is the role of an insurance surveyor/loss assessor?
options: To independently assess the cause and extent of a loss
questions.explanation: Surveyors are independent professionals who investigate and quantify the extent of loss in general insurance claims over a certain value.

questions.text: The principle of Causa Proxima is used to determine:
options: The primary, effective cause of the loss
The financial status of the proposer
questions.explanation: Causa Proxima helps determine if a loss was caused by an insured peril or an excluded peril in a chain of events.

questions.text: In marine insurance, 'Jettison' means:
options: Voluntary throwing of cargo overboard to lighten and save the ship
questions.explanation: Jettison is a deliberate act of throwing goods overboard during an emergency to prevent the vessel from sinking.

questions.text: Consequential loss insurance is also commonly known as:
questions.explanation: Consequential loss insurance covers the loss of revenue and business profits resulting from a fire or other insured peril.

questions.text: Which authority issues the license to act as an insurance agent in India?
options: Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India (IRDAI)
questions.explanation: IRDAI is the sole authority empowered to grant, renew, or cancel licenses for insurance intermediaries in India.

questions.text: What is the minimum age requirement to become an insurance agent in India?
questions.explanation: An individual must be at least 18 years old and have the required educational qualifications to apply for an agency license.

questions.text: A void contract in insurance is one that is:
options: Unenforceable by law from the beginning
Valid only in a court of law
questions.explanation: A void contract has no legal standing and is treated as if it never existed (e.g., insurance taken without insurable interest).

questions.text: What does 'Sum Insured' represent in a general insurance policy?
options: The total premium to be paid
The maximum liability of the insurer in case of a loss
questions.explanation: The Sum Insured is the maximum amount the insurer is liable to pay for any loss during the policy period.

questions.text: Which of these is NOT a function of the insurance agent?
options: Assisting in filling the proposal form
Underwriting the risk and setting the premium
questions.explanation: Underwriting (assessing risk and setting prices) is the function of the insurance company, not the agent.

questions.text: When does the risk coverage typically begin in general insurance?
options: When the proposal form is printed
Only when the premium is received by the insurer
When the agent visits the customer
When the customer signs the claim form
questions.explanation: Section 64VB of the Insurance Act states that risk cover cannot commence until the premium is received by the insurer.
